title: The Two Philosophies and What Each Claims
html:
Two answers to one question

Every trade is a bet about price. The two great schools of market analysis disagree about where the information that moves price lives. Fundamental analysis (FA) says value comes from the business behind the ticker: its earnings, cash flows, debts, and growth. Technical analysis (TA) says everything you need is already in the price and volume record, because the chart is the aggregate footprint of every buyer and seller who has acted.

These are not just different toolkits; they rest on different claims about reality. Read them as testable assertions, not slogans.

core claims, side by side
Fundamental analysis claims: 1. A security has an intrinsic value set by future cash flows. 2. Price can diverge from value, sometimes for years. 3. Over the long run, price converges to value. Edge = estimating value better than the crowd. Technical analysis claims: 1. Price discounts everything — all known facts are in it. 2. Prices move in trends and patterns driven by human behaviour. 3. History rhymes because crowd psychology repeats. Edge = reading the tape and managing risk better than the crowd.

The 30% drop: two interrogations

Imagine ACME falls 30% in three weeks. Same chart, two analysts, two completely different questions.

The chart above shows the slide and the moment price slices through a support shelf near 96 that had held twice before. The fundamentalist ignores the line. She asks: did anything about the business change? If earnings guidance was cut 35%, a 30% price drop may be rational — or even insufficient. If nothing changed and only sentiment soured, she sees a sale. Her question is "is it cheap?"

The technician barely cares why. He sees that a level which previously attracted buyers has failed, volume expanded on the break, and price is now below a falling 50-day average. To him the message is structural: supply has overwhelmed demand and the path of least resistance is down until proven otherwise. His question is "did support hold?" — and it did not.

Insight.
Notice they are not really arguing. The fundamentalist is answering what to own; the technician is answering when and at what risk. The conflict is mostly an illusion created by people who only own one tool.

What each is actually trying to predict

This is the cleanest way to keep them straight: they forecast different variables over different horizons.

DimensionFundamentalTechnical
PredictsLong-run value / fair priceNear-term direction & volatility
HorizonQuarters to yearsMinutes to months
Primary dataFinancial statements, macroPrice, volume, time
Fails whenTiming — value takes years to be recognisedRegime change — old patterns break
Key risk"Value trap" (cheap stays cheap)"Whipsaw" (false signal)

Why the dispute persists

If one school were simply right, the other would have died decades ago. Both survive because markets are layered. On a quiet Tuesday, an index can drift on order-flow and positioning with no fundamental news at all — a technical environment. On an earnings night, a stock gaps 12% on a number no chart predicted — a fundamental environment. The same instrument is governed by different forces at different moments, which is precisely why professionals refuse to pick a side (Lesson 4.5).

Definition.
Intrinsic value is the present value of all cash a business will return to owners. Price is what the last marginal buyer and seller agreed on. FA trades the gap between them; TA trades the path of price itself.
Pitfall.
Beginners adopt a school as an identity ("I'm a chartist," "I'm a value guy") and then dismiss disconfirming evidence. The market does not reward loyalty to a method — only loyalty to reality. Hold both frameworks loosely.
▸Try It

Goal: feel the two questions on one event.

  1. Pick a stock that recently moved more than 15% in a week.
  2. Write the fundamentalist question: did earnings, guidance, or the balance sheet change enough to justify the move?
  3. Write the technician question: did the move break or hold a level that mattered before?
  4. Decide which question your trading horizon makes you care about more — and notice that the other still matters.

title: The Efficient-Market Hypothesis and Its Critics
html:
The idea that haunts every analyst

If markets instantly and correctly price all information, then no analysis — technical or fundamental — can produce a durable edge. That uncomfortable claim is the Efficient-Market Hypothesis (EMH), formalised by Eugene Fama in 1970. You cannot honestly study analysis without first staring it down, because EMH is the null hypothesis your edge must beat.

Definition.
A market is efficient if prices fully reflect available information, so the expected risk-adjusted return from trading on that information is zero. Prices then move only on genuinely new (unpredictable) news — a random walk.

The three forms

Fama split the hypothesis into three strengths, defined by which information set is already in the price. Each form, if true, kills a different kind of analysis.

the three forms of EMH
WEAK form — price reflects all past prices & volume. If true: technical analysis cannot work. SEMI-STRONG form — price reflects all public information (filings, news). If true: fundamental analysis on public data cannot work. STRONG form — price reflects everything, including private/insider info. If true: even insiders cannot beat the market. Almost nobody believes the strong form — insiders demonstrably profit.

Notice the structure: the weak form is the direct enemy of TA, the semi-strong form is the enemy of FA, and the strong form is so extreme that insider-trading prosecutions disprove it daily. The real debate lives in the weak and semi-strong forms.

The case FOR efficiency

It is not a strawman; the evidence is genuinely strong. Most active fund managers underperform a cheap index fund over 10–15 years after fees — roughly 80–90% in long-horizon studies. If beating the market were easy, that number would be reversed. Markets also react fast: major public news is typically reflected in price within seconds, leaving little for a retail analyst to harvest.

The serious critiques

But "hard to beat" is not "impossible to beat," and several findings sit awkwardly with strict efficiency.

The parabola above is the kind of episode critics point to. A perfectly efficient price should not melt up 60% and then collapse without proportionate news — yet bubbles and crashes recur. Consider the headline anomalies:

  • The 1987 crash. On 19 October 1987 the Dow fell about 22.6% in a single day with no commensurate news. What fundamental fact changed by a fifth overnight? Robert Shiller's surveys found the move was driven by feedback and portfolio insurance, not information — hard to square with rational pricing.
  • Momentum. Jegadeesh and Titman (1993) showed that stocks which outperformed over 3–12 months keep outperforming for a while. That is a past-price signal predicting future returns — a direct violation of the weak form, and it persists across decades and countries.
  • Value & size. Cheap (low price-to-book) and small stocks have historically earned premiums. Fama himself absorbed these into multi-factor models — arguably saving EMH by redefining "risk."
  • Excess volatility. Shiller showed prices swing far more than the discounted stream of future dividends ever did — markets are too jumpy to be purely rational.
Insight.
The honest synthesis, from Andrew Lo's Adaptive Markets Hypothesis: markets are mostly efficient but not perfectly, and the inefficiencies are real, small, crowded, and decay as people exploit them. Edge exists — it is just scarce, competitive, and perishable.

What this means for you

EMH should make you humble, not paralysed. Three working conclusions:

  1. The strong form is false — so private edges (genuine information, speed, structure) can pay.
  2. The semi-strong form is mostly true — so reading a public 10-K everyone has read rarely gives an edge by itself; the edge is in superior interpretation.
  3. The weak form is leaky — momentum survives, which is why technical trend-following is not pure superstition.
Pitfall.
Anomalies shrink after publication. The value and size premiums weakened markedly after academics broadcast them in the 1990s. Any edge you find that is easy to describe is probably already being arbitraged. Assume your signal is decaying.
▸Try It

Goal: locate your strategy against the three forms.

  1. Write down one strategy you believe in (e.g., "buy 200-day breakouts").
  2. Identify which EMH form it must violate to work (this one violates the weak form).
  3. Ask: why hasn't this been arbitraged away? Name the real reason — a behavioural bias, a structural constraint, or a risk premium.
  4. If you can't name one, treat the edge as suspect.

title: What Technical Analysis Is Genuinely Good and Bad At
html:
Separating the signal from the folklore

Technical analysis carries a lot of baggage — fans who believe candlestick names have magic, and skeptics who dismiss the whole field as astrology. The truth is in between and is specific: TA is excellent at a few things and weak at others, and the evidence lets us say which is which.

Where TA genuinely adds value

1. Defining risk with precision

This is TA's strongest, least disputed contribution. A chart gives you objective, pre-decided exit levels. You do not need to believe a trendline predicts anything to use it as a line in the sand that says "I was wrong."

In the chart, the rising trendline connects the swing lows. Whatever you think of its predictive power, it gives you a clean, unambiguous stop: if price closes decisively below it, the up-structure is broken and you exit. That converts a vague feeling into a measurable risk of, say, 4% — which lets you size the position correctly. FA gives you no such line; "it's undervalued" tells you nothing about where to bail.

2. Timing entries and exits

Fundamentals tell you what; they are nearly silent on when. TA fills that gap. Waiting for a pullback to a moving average, or for a breakout to confirm, can dramatically improve entry price and reduce time spent underwater.

3. Reading liquidity and order flow

Support, resistance, and volume are real reflections of where supply and demand cluster. A high-volume breakout above a multi-month resistance reveals that a price ceiling has been absorbed — genuine information about positioning, not myth.

Insight.
The strongest empirical support is for trend / momentum and volume confirmation. Decades of data across asset classes show price persistence is real. If you keep one technical idea, keep "the trend is your friend until it bends" — it has the receipts.

Where TA is weak — and the evidence is thin

1. Complex named patterns as predictions

The famous chart patterns sound authoritative but test poorly out-of-sample. A head-and-shoulders is supposed to signal reversal; in controlled studies its hit rate hovers near a coin flip once you account for the many that fail or are drawn after the fact.

The pattern above is gorgeous in hindsight. The problem is twofold: hindsight bias (you only notice the ones that completed) and subjectivity (two analysts draw the neckline differently). A 'prediction' that fails roughly half the time is not useless, but it is not the precise forecast its name implies — treat it as a risk map, not a crystal ball.

TA scorecard — by the evidence
STRONG evidence + Trend / momentum persistence + Support & resistance as risk levels + Volume confirming a move WEAK / MIXED evidence ~ Named reversal patterns (H&S, double tops) ~ Oscillator overbought/oversold as standalone signals ~ Precise price 'targets' from pattern height NO credible evidence x Elliott Wave exact counts, Fibonacci to the cent Rule of thumb: the more precise the claim, the weaker the evidence.

2. Oscillators in a trend

An RSI screaming "overbought" in a powerful uptrend is a classic trap — strong trends stay overbought for weeks. Oscillators describe momentum; they do not, by themselves, time reversals.

3. Over-fitting and self-deception

With enough indicators and parameters you can always find a setting that 'worked' on the past. This is the deepest danger in TA: it is so flexible that it can manufacture confidence with no real edge.

Pitfall.
Curve-fitting. If your backtest improved every time you added an indicator, you are probably fitting noise. A robust technical edge is usually simple (one or two rules) and survives on data it was never tuned on.
Do this.
Use TA for what it's strong at — risk levels, timing, trend, volume — and demote the ornate pattern-naming to context. Anchor every technical trade to a hard invalidation level before you enter.
▸Try It

Goal: separate TA's risk-management value from its prediction claims.

  1. On any chart, draw the most recent swing low or trendline.
  2. Write the exact price where your trade idea is "wrong" — that's your stop.
  3. Now resist drawing a fancy pattern. Just ask: is price trending up, down, or ranging, and is volume confirming?
  4. Notice you've extracted TA's reliable value without relying on a single named pattern.

title: What Fundamental Analysis Is Genuinely Good and Bad At
html:
The discipline of asking what a thing is worth

Fundamental analysis answers a question technicals never even pose: what is this business actually worth, and can it survive? Done well, it is the difference between owning a productive asset and owning a ticker symbol you hope someone pays more for. But its strengths and weaknesses are as specific as TA's, and the biggest weakness is one word: timing.

Where FA genuinely adds value

1. Estimating long-run value

Over years, prices really do track earnings and cash flow. A business compounding free cash flow at 15% a year will, eventually, drag its stock price up with it. FA is the only toolkit that can tell you whether the thing you're buying generates value at all.

a quick valuation sketch — ACME
Inputs Price = $60 EPS (this year) = $5 Earnings growth ~ 10%/yr Debt/Equity = 0.3 (low) P/E = Price ÷ EPS = 60 ÷ 5 = 12× Peers trade at 18×. Same growth, less debt. PEG = P/E ÷ growth = 12 ÷ 10 = 1.2 Below the ~2 where peers sit → relatively cheap. Read: a solvent business priced below comparable peers. Unknown: WHEN the market agrees. FA is silent on that.

That sketch shows FA's power and its hole in one frame. It can tell you ACME is statistically cheap and financially sound — but nothing in the math says whether the re-rating happens next month or in three years.

2. Judging solvency and survival

This is FA's quiet superpower and TA's blind spot. A chart cannot see a debt wall. Reading the balance sheet — leverage, interest coverage, cash runway — is how you avoid owning a company that goes to zero. No trendline warns you a firm can't refinance; the footnotes do.

3. Sizing the margin of safety

Buying at a discount to conservative value gives you a cushion against being wrong. That cushion is a fundamental concept with no technical equivalent.

Where FA falls short

1. Timing — the value trap

This is the central, brutal weakness. Being right about value and wrong about when can be financially indistinguishable from being wrong, period.

The chart is the value investor's nightmare: ACME is genuinely undervalued at $60, and the analyst is right — but the stock grinds sideways in a $55–$66 box for three years while the thesis sits dead money. Capital that could have compounded elsewhere is trapped waiting for a catalyst that hasn't arrived. "The market can stay irrational longer than you can stay solvent" is the FA practitioner's epitaph.

Pitfall.
The value trap: a stock is cheap because it deserves to be — eroding moat, dying industry, hidden liability. Cheapness alone is not a thesis. Without a catalyst (new management, a buyback, a cycle turn), "cheap" can mean "cheap forever."

2. Garbage-in estimates

A discounted cash flow model is only as good as its assumptions, and small changes in the growth or discount rate swing the output enormously. False precision is FA's vanity: a model outputting "intrinsic value = $84.37" is pretending to a confidence it does not have.

3. Reflexivity and narrative

Fundamentals are not fully independent of price. A falling stock can raise a company's cost of capital and actually worsen the fundamentals — Soros's reflexivity. The clean separation of value and price is an idealisation.

Insight.
FA and TA fail in opposite places, which is why they pair so well. FA is strong on what and whether it survives, weak on when. TA is strong on when and risk levels, weak on what it's worth. Each covers the other's blind spot.
FA is good atFA is bad at
Long-run valueTiming the realisation
Solvency / survivalShort-term moves
Margin of safetyFalse precision in models
Avoiding zerosReflexive feedback with price
▸Try It

Goal: stress-test a 'cheap' stock for the value trap.

  1. Find a stock with a low P/E relative to its peers.
  2. Ask why it's cheap — list one bullish reason and one bearish reason the market might be right.
  3. Name a concrete catalyst that would force a re-rating, and a rough timeframe.
  4. If you can't name a catalyst, label it a potential value trap and move on.

title: Timeframe, and How Professionals Blend the Two
html:
Timeframe decides the winner

The endless TA-vs-FA debate dissolves the moment you specify a holding period. The two methods do not compete across all horizons — each dominates a different one. Get the horizon right and the choice of tool follows automatically.

which force dominates, by horizon
Seconds → days flow, sentiment, levels → TA dominates Fundamentals don't change minute to minute; positioning does. Weeks → months mix of both → blend Trends + catalysts both matter on this scale. Years earnings & cash flow → FA dominates Over years price tracks value; noise averages out.

Benjamin Graham's line captures it: "In the short run the market is a voting machine; in the long run it is a weighing machine." TA reads the voting; FA estimates the weight. A day trader who ignores the tape is handicapped; a decade-long investor who frets over a 5-day moving average is fooling himself with noise.

How professionals actually blend them

Serious shops almost never pick a side. The dominant pattern is a clean division of labour: fundamentals choose the security; technicals choose the moment. FA narrows a universe of thousands to a short list of businesses worth owning; TA decides the entry, the stop, and the size.

This is the textbook blend in one chart. A fund's analysts decide ACME is undervalued — that's the FA selection. But rather than buy at any price, the trading desk waits for a pullback to the rising 50-day moving average and buys into that dip. The fundamental work answers what and whether; the technical work answers where and how much risk. The stop sits just below the moving average, so a clean invalidation level is defined the instant they enter.

Do this.
Adopt the two-question protocol on every trade: (1) FA: is this worth owning / is the direction justified? If no, stop. (2) TA: is now a good entry, and where am I wrong? If no clean level, wait. You only act when both answer yes.

Different professionals, different blends

  • Value / long-only funds. Heavily FA for selection; light TA to avoid catching a falling knife and to scale in. Holding period years — FA leads.
  • Systematic trend funds (CTAs). Almost pure TA — they trade price signals across hundreds of markets and don't model individual fundamentals at all. Holding period weeks to months.
  • Global macro. A genuine fusion: a fundamental thesis ("this currency is overvalued and the central bank must cut") expressed only when the chart confirms the move has begun — fundamentals for the idea, technicals for timing and risk.
  • Event-driven / catalysts. FA identifies the catalyst (merger, spin-off, earnings inflection); TA frames the risk around the event.
Insight.
The best blend reflects the failure modes from Lessons 4.3–4.4. FA's blind spot is timing; TA's blind spot is value and solvency. Bolting them together patches both holes — FA stops you buying a chart of a company about to go bankrupt; TA stops you buying a 'cheap' stock the day before it falls another 30%.

An honest word on what works

After all five lessons, the evidence-based summary is uncomfortable but liberating:

  1. Markets are mostly efficient. Easy, well-known edges are arbitraged away. Assume your edge is small and perishable.
  2. Some inefficiencies are real. Trend/momentum (a technical effect) and value (a fundamental effect) have persisted across decades — these are the most defensible edges.
  3. Risk management beats prediction. The most reliable contribution of TA is not forecasting but defining where you're wrong. The most reliable contribution of FA is not pinpoint valuation but avoiding ruin.
  4. Process over method. Survivors aren't the ones with the 'right' school; they're the ones who size positions, honour stops, and stay solvent long enough for a small edge to compound.
Pitfall.
Don't blend into mush. "Use both" can become an excuse to override a stop because "the fundamentals are still good," or to abandon a thesis because of one red candle. Define in advance which tool governs which decision: FA for what to own, TA for when and how much risk — and don't let them veto each other's job.
▸Try It

Goal: run one trade through the professional blend.

  1. State your holding period in plain words (days? months? years?). Let it tell you which tool leads.
  2. FA step: write one sentence on why the security is worth owning or the direction is justified.
  3. TA step: identify a specific entry trigger (e.g., pullback to the 50-day MA) and the exact stop level below it.
  4. Size the position from the distance to that stop — and only enter if both the FA and TA answers are yes.
